svg.text: confidentiality = ML-KEM-768 · identity = Ed25519 + Falcon-512 (hybrid) · consensus = Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 (hybrid)

g.text: src/circuit.c:1011–1142 · src/crypto.c:566–579 · classical auth_tag proves relay identity; symmetric keys derive from ML-KEM shared secret ONLY
auth_tag = BLAKE2b-MAC( ck, "moor-cke-verify" ‖ relay_id ‖ client_eph ‖ relay_eph ) ← identity proof only
CELL_KEM_CT [ ML-KEM-768 ciphertext · 1088 B · fragmented ≥3 cells ]
client: (ct, ss) = ML-KEM.Encaps(relay.kem_pk) relay: ss = ML-KEM.Decaps(ct, relay.kem_sk)
confidentiality root = ML-KEM shared secret. A future CRQC breaking X25519 cannot retroactively decrypt any recorded
cell, because session keys never mix X25519 material into the KDF. The classical auth_tag only binds relay identity —
it authenticates, it does not encrypt (phase-3 → replace with Falcon in CREATED_PQ).
2 · .moor v3 address + HS descriptor — PQ commitment + dual signature
src/hidden_service.c:161–210 (address) · desc_sign_serialize / desc_sign_verify · address binds BOTH kem_pk AND falcon_pk
content_hash = BLAKE2b( blinded_pk · intro_points · pow_seed · pow_diff · revision )
Ed25519.Verify( service_pk, to_sign, ed_sig ) ∧ Falcon.Verify( falcon_pk, to_sign, fa_sig )
Why commit to PQ keys IN the address?
The old .moor address bound only the Ed25519 service_pk. A future CRQC that breaks Ed25519 could forge a new descriptor
under a different kem_pk / falcon_pk and steal all client connects. v3 folds H( kem_pk ‖ falcon_pk ) into the address →
an attacker must break the HASH, not just Ed25519.
Ed25519 alone dies to a CRQC. Falcon alone is still young. Signing over the SAME to_sign[96] with both algorithms means
an attacker needs BOTH primitives broken to forge. Mismatched ed/falcon sigs over the same bytes means substitution is
3 · Hidden-service connect — sealed INTRODUCE, hybrid e2e, atomic nonce = 0
(1) fetch descriptor · verify ed_sig ∧ falcon_sig over to_sign · PQ commitment vs address
(2) 3-hop circuit to RP + random 20 B cookie
PQ-sealed-blob = moor_crypto_pq_seal( rp_node_id(32) ‖ cookie(20) ‖ client_eph_pk(32), HS.kem_pk )
intro relay sees opaque 1120 B — can't decrypt, can't correlate RP, can't substitute
(4) intro relay forwards — blob stays sealed end-to-end
encap KEM → send CELL_E2E_KEM_CT → flip e2e_active @ nonce = 0
key = BLAKE2b( x25519_ss ‖ kem_ss ) → flip e2e_active @ nonce = 0
no cell EVER encrypted with X25519-only keys
(a) INTRODUCE1 is PQ-sealed to the HS's kem_pk — intro relay cannot decrypt rp_node_id or cookie, so it can't collude to correlate client ↔ HS.
(b) e2e rendezvous key = HYBRID(X25519 ‖ ML-KEM) — an attacker must break BOTH primitives to decrypt rendezvous traffic; record-now-decrypt-later fails.
(c) e2e_active flips only AFTER the KEM CT round-trip at nonce = 0 — there is no window where cells are sent with the X25519-only key and upgraded later.
src/directory.c:1102–1159 (moor_consensus_verify_hybrid) · src/config.c:100–144 (hardcoded DA pks) · classical-only verify DELETED
A CRQC-break of Ed25519 alone cannot forge a consensus that clients accept. Equally, an undiscovered ML-DSA flaw cannot —
Ed25519 is an independent anchor. No single-primitive failure replaces the network's trust root.
